coredata.dc:title: Label/immobilization-free Cas12a-based electrochemiluminescence biosensor for sensitive DNA detection
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Honghong
Hang, Xiaomin
Wang, Huiyi
Peng, Jiaxin
Yu, Haoming
Wang, Li
coredata.dc:description: Electrochemiluminescence (ECL) is one of the most sensitive techniques in the field of diagnostics. However, they typically require luminescent labeling and electrode surface biological modification, which is a time-consuming and laborious process involving multiple steps and may also lead to low reaction efficiency. Fabricating label/modification-free biosensors has become one of the most attractive parts for simplifying the ECL assays. In this work, the ECL luminophores carbon dots (CDs) were encapsulated in DNA hydrogel in situ by a simple rolling circle amplification (RCA) reaction. Upon binding of the target DNA, active Cas12a induces a collateral cleavage of the hydrogel's ssDNA backbone, resulting in a programmable degradation of the hydrogel and the release of CDs. By directly measuring the released CDs ECL, a simple and rapid label/modification-free detection of the target HPV-16 was realized. It is noted that this method allowed for 0.63 pM HPV-16 DNA detection without any amplification step, and it could take only ∼60 min for a fast test of a human serum sample. These results showed that our label/modification-free ECL biosensor has great potential for use in simple, rapid, and sensitive point-of-care (POC) detection.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Electrochemiluminescence
Biosensor
CRISPR/Cas12a
DNA hydrogel
Label/modification-free

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Practical application of the constructed sensor

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Characterization of CDs-DNA hydrogel. (A) TEM image of CDs. (B) SEM image of DNA hydrogel. (C) UV–vis absorption spectrum and (D) Fluorescence spectrum of the CDs-DNA hydrogel system under different conditions. (Primer: final, 5 μM; ssDNA1 and ssDNA2: final, 10 μM; Cas12a: final, 50 nM; crRNA: final, 100 nM; target: initial, 10 nM; Fluorescence excitation at 416 nm).

Feasibility analysis. (A) PAGE analysis of different samples. (Left) lane 1: primer; lane 2: PC; lane 3: PC/primer + T4 DNA ligase; lane 4: PC/primer + T4 ligase DNA + Exo I + Exo III. (Right) lane 1: DNA hydrogel; lane 2: DNA hydrogel + Cas12a/crRNA; lane 3: DNA hydrogel + Cas12a/crRNA + HPV-16 DNA. The total volume of all samples was 10 μL, the final concentration of DNA was 5 μM and the target was 10 nM. (B) ECL response of various samples. a) Bare GE; b) DNA hydrogel without CDs + Cas12a/crRNA + HPV-16 DNA; c) CDs-DNA hydrogel + crRNA + HPV-16 DNA; d) CDs-DNA hydrogel + Cas12a + HPV-16 DNA; e) CDs-DNA hydrogel + Cas12a/crRNA; f) CDs-DNA hydrogel + Cas12a/crRNA + HPV-16 DNA. (Primer: final, 5 μM; ssDNA1 and ssDNA1: final, 10 μM; Cas12a: final, 50 nM; crRNA: final, 100 nM; HPV-16: initial, 10 nM).

(A) PAGE images of DNA-hydrogel cleaved by activated CRISPR/Cas12a at different times. (B) Working mechanism of DNA hydrogel disassembly in response to the target. (C) Visual fluorescence observation and (D) Corresponding fluorescence intensity of CDs released after DNA hydrogel disaggregation. HPV-16: 1 μM. Error bar means SD (n = 3).

Optimization of the experimental conditions. (A) ECL signals for different PC/primer concentrations. (B) ECL signals using different ratios of Cas12a to crRNA. (C) The incubation time of Cas12a/crRNA, HPV-16 DNA, and CDs-DNA hydrogel. (D) The cleavage temperature of Cas12a. HPV-16 10 nM. Error bar means SD (n = 3).

(A) ECL curves responding to 0, 1 pM, 10 pM, 50 pM, 100 pM, 500 pM, 1 nM, 10 nM, and 100 nM HPV-16 DNA. (B) Linear fitting of the Δ ECL and the logarithmic concentration of HPV-16 DNA. (C) Specificity of HPV-16 DNA detection (Target: 100 pM; HPV-18, HPV-33, HPV-58, HBV DNA: 500 pM). (D) Reproducibility of HPV-16 DNA detection. Error bar means SD (n = 3).

Schematic illustration of the label/immobilization-free ECL biosensor for HPV-16 DNA detection. (A) The preparation of CDs-DNA hydrogel. (B) The detection of HPV-16 DNA based on Cas12a collaterally cleaves ssDNA sequence from CDs-DNA hydrogel.

head.ce:title: Label/immobilization-free Cas12a-based electrochemiluminescence biosensor for sensitive DNA detection

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Pharmacy, Jiangsu University, Zhenjiang, 212013, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Pharmacy, Jiangsu University, Zhenjiang 212013, P. R. China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of the Environment and Safety Engineering, Jiangsu University, Zhenjiang, 212013, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of the Environment and Safety Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of the Environment and Safety Engineering, Jiangsu University, Zhenjiang 212013, P. R. China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Electrochemiluminescence (ECL) is one of the most sensitive techniques in the field of diagnostics. However, they typically require luminescent labeling and electrode surface biological modification, which is a time-consuming and laborious process involving multiple steps and may also lead to low reaction efficiency. Fabricating label/modification-free biosensors has become one of the most attractive parts for simplifying the ECL assays. In this work, the ECL luminophores carbon dots (CDs) were encapsulated in DNA hydrogel in situ by a simple rolling circle amplification (RCA) reaction. Upon binding of the target DNA, active Cas12a induces a collateral cleavage of the hydrogel's ssDNA backbone, resulting in a programmable degradation of the hydrogel and the release of CDs. By directly measuring the released CDs ECL, a simple and rapid label/modification-free detection of the target HPV-16 was realized. It is noted that this method allowed for 0.63 pM HPV-16 DNA detection without any amplification step, and it could take only ∼60 min for a fast test of a human serum sample. These results showed that our label/modification-free ECL biosensor has great potential for use in simple, rapid, and sensitive point-of-care (POC) detection.

A novel ECL sensing platform integrating CRISPR/Cas12a and DNA hydrogel technology enabled label/immobilization-free sensitive detection of HPV-16 DNA.
Image 1

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Electrochemiluminescence (ECL) is a luminescent process induced by an electrochemical reaction, which combines the advantages of electrochemical and spectroscopic methods, and has the characteristics of low background, high sensitivity, high selectivity, fast response, and broad dynamic range [1–4]. In addition to these remarkable advantages, this technology also allows the utilization of simplified optical architectures as well as miniaturized microelectronic systems, being very appealing for the development of cheap point-of-care (POC) detection [5,6]. Up to now, ECL methods have been widely used in measuring a range of proteins, microRNAs, DNA, enzymes, and enzyme substrates [7–9]. A typical ECL biosensor requires the biomolecular to be immobilized on the electrode surface [10,11]. However, the immobilization procedure not only needs repeated operations but has the drawbacks of low hybridization and cleavage efficiency caused by steric hindrance effect and high salt concentration on the solid surface. Moreover, most of the ECL sensors also suffered from the strict, complex, and high-cost labeling steps for signal readout [12,13]. There has been a great interest in developing label-free or modification-free ECL biosensors to facilitate the detection of targets in the past decade. Scientists have, to date, developed a series of label-free or modification-free biosensors to simplify the ECL assays [14–18]. However, they still face the challenge of being simultaneously label-free and modification-free. Furthermore, for these methods, an addition of target amplification is needed to achieve high sensitivity, and this makes the detection process further complicated [19,20]. In addition, they, in some cases, require a specially designed, negative-charged electrode or positive-charged nanomaterials. Therefore, manufacturing a facile, label-free, and immobilization-free ECL sensor for bioanalysis applications is meaningful.

Over the last few years, with the rapid progression of gene editing, clustered regularly interspaced short palindromic repeats (CRISPR) technology has become a promising tool for bioassays [21–23]. In particular, the more recently discovered Cas12a and Cas13a proteins exhibit trans-cleavage activity, which enables the assay ultrasensitive, fast, and easy to use, making them ideally suited for POC and resource-limited settings [24–28]. Although significant progress has been made, traditional CRISPR detection typically requires the use of a fluorophore-quencher pair to label DNA/RNA reporter for signal readout, which is time-consuming and costly [29]. Besides, since each biomolecular is labeled with only one fluorophore, it is difficult to detect trace targets by a single Cas protein, an additional pre-amplification step is commonly needed to achieve desirable detection sensitivity [30–32]. The preamplification is error-prone, requires an elaborate primers design, and would prolong the detection time [33]. Therefore, the development of label-free CRISPR biosensors is much more attractive to further simplify the assay. Very recently, it is exciting that scientists have demonstrated the possibility of fabrication of CRISPR-based label-free biosensors. One of the most impressive examples is using the Cas12a-responsive DNA hydrogel, wherein the signal molecules are initially loaded in situ. In the case of sensing, Cas12a induces a collateral cleavage of the ssDNA backbone of hydrogel upon binding of the target DNA, resulting in programmable degradation of the material and the release of payloads. By measuring the signal in the intrinsic physical and chemical parameters of the released materials, a simple and rapid detection of the target was realized [34]. It is noted that the synthesis procedure of this DNA hydrogel is simple and versatile. This inspires scientists to utilize this DNA hydrogel for developing several types of CRISPR biosensors including colorimetry, fluorescence, and electrochemistry for label-free POC testing [35–37]. However, such an attractive material has not yet been explored for ECL sensing applications.

In this work, we proposed a novel label/immobilization-free ECL sensing platform based on Cas12a-responsive DNA hydrogel for DNA detection. Specifically, the specific reporter—DNA hydrogel containing luminescent carbon dots (CDs) was synthesized via double-rolling circle amplification (RCA). HPV-16, a major cause of cervical cancer [38–40], was adopted as a model target for detection. In the detection procedure, the presence of HPV-16 DNA induced the CRISPR/Cas12a-mediated enzymatic cleavage reaction, leading to the trans-cleavage of the single-strand DNA (ssDNA) skeleton of the hydrogel and the CDs release, enabling us to directly detect the released CDs ECL signal without any electrode treatment (Scheme 1 ). It is noted that ECL measurement endowed this method with high sensitivity, achieving a detection limit of 0.63 pM. The detection platform also offered exact specificity by directly utilizing the intrinsic crRNA complementation capability of CRISPR without any amplification. In addition to these remarkable advantages, this sensing approach also provided a wide detection range and fast response, as well as the reliability and reproducibility of the ECL sensor without the label/immobilization step.

Phi29 DNA polymerase derived from Bacillus stearothermophilus and 10 × reaction buffer, T4 DNA Ligase, and 10 × ligation buffer were offered by Beyotime Biotechnology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). LbCas12a and 10 × TOLO buffer were bought from Tolo Biotech (Shanghai, China). Urea and citric acid were purchased from Xiensi Aupu De Technology Co., Ltd. (Tianjin, China). Exonuclease I (Exo I), Exonuclease III (Exo III), DEPC-treated water, 4S GelRed Nucleic acid dye, DNA Marker, deoxynucleotide triphosphates (dNTPs), and all the DNA oligonucleotides purified by HPLC were from Sangon (Shanghai, China), crRNA purified by HPLC was purchased from Genepharma (Shanghai, China). The corresponding oligonucleotide sequences were listed in Supporting Information (Table S1).

Polyacrylamide gel electrophoresis (PAGE) experiments were carried out by a DYY-6C electrophoresis analyzer (Beijing, China). The gels were stained with 4S GelRed Nucleic acid dye and imaged by a ZF-90 UV transmission reflectance analyzer (JIHUI, China). Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were obtained by a H-7800 transmission electron microscope (HITACHI, Japan). Scanning electron microscope (SEM) images were studied by a Hitachi-S4800 FESEM (HITACHI, Japan). The ECL instrument containing the three-electrode system (working electrode, Ag/AgCl reference electrode, platinum wire counter electrode) was purchased from Remex Analysis Instruments Co. Ltd. (Xi'an, China). Phosphate buffer (pH 7.4) containing 0.1 M K2S2O8 was used as the co-reactant. Fluorescence intensity was measured by a standard microplate reader (BioTek, USA).
CDs were synthesized from the raw materials citric acid and urea by a microwave-assisted method, and the specific synthesis process referred to the previous article of our group [41].

The CDs-DNA hydrogel was prepared by a two-step RCA reaction according to the previous literature with some modifications [42,43]. To perform the one-step RCA, first combined 1 μL of 100 μM padlock probe (PC) and 1 μL of 100 μM primer, 2 μL of 10 × ligation buffer with 15.5 μL of DEPC-treated water, reacted at 37 °C for 1 h. Then 0.5 μL of T4 DNA ligase (25 U μL−1) was added and incubated at room temperature for 3 h, 65 °C for 10 min to obtain circular DNA (circ-DNA). Subsequently, the circ-DNA were incubated with dNTPs (8 μL, 25 mM), NaCl (2 μL, 0.8 M), BSA (2 μL, 1 mg mL−1), phi29 DNA polymerase (1 μL, 10 U), 10 × reaction buffer (7 μL), and 30 μL of DEPC-treated water at 30 °C for 4 h. For the two-step RCA, the above product was mixed with 10 μL of CDs, and then 2 μL each of 100 μM specific primers (ssDNA1 and ssDNA2) were introduced every 2 h (until the final concentration of 10 μM). The reaction mixture was incubated for 16 h at 30 °C. The CDs-DNA hydrogel was obtained and washed five times with PBS, and stored at 4 °C until use.

The target-responsive CRISPR/Cas12a reaction (30 μL) was performed by incubation of 5 μL of HPV-16 DNA, Cas12a/crRNA (Cas12a: final, 50 nM, crRNA: final, 100 nM), pre-prepared CDs-DNA hydrogel, 3 μL of 10 × TOLO buffer and DEPC-treated water at 37 °C for 60 min to release CDs into aqueous phase. A three-electrode system with a gold electrode as the working electrode and K2S2O8 as a co-reactant was used to directly measure the ECL signal of CDs in the aqueous phase and the potential range from 0 ∼ −1.6 V, photomultiplier tube (PMT) was set at 600 V.

The label/modification-free ECL biosensor preparation procedure and the target detection are outlined in Scheme 1. The CDs ECL emitter is incorporated into the DNA hydrogel by a two-step RCA reaction (Scheme 1A, Fig. S1). First, the DNA network consists of an ultra-long ssDNA strand produced by RCA and two other long DNA strands produced by the addition of ssDNA1 and ssDNA2 on this basis, which are wound and self-assembled. The CDs are water-soluble, and they have many functional groups such as hydroxyl, carboxyl, and amino groups on the surfaces, which make them interact with bases on DNA strands through forming hydrogen bonds or electrostatic interactions. Ultra-long DNA strands contain alternately arranged complementary sequences, so the resulting multiple DNA strands cross-link each other to form 3D DNA networks, leading to the encapsulation of CDs. Subsequent CDs release is triggered by CRISPR/Cas12a-induced DNA hydrogel network disassembly (Scheme 1B). In detail, the Cas12a protein and crRNA initially form a functional ribonucleoprotein (RNP) which can bind the target with a complementary sequence to the crRNA spacer region adjacent to an A/T-rich PAM region. Once bound to a target HPV-16 dsDNA, the Cas12a RNP forms a double-strand structure (R-loop) between complementary sequences. The Cas12a RNP thus activated is capable of trans-cleavage which indiscriminately degrades ssDNA sequences. As a result, the ssDNA backbones in the DNA hydrogel network are disassembled into fragments, thereby the gel degrades and CDs release into the aqueous supernatant, and it may be directly detected by an ECL station.

TEM image of the CDs was shown in Fig. 1 A, CDs were spherical, less than 10 nm in diameter, well dispersed, and had strong fluorescence emission at 527 nm ((Fig. S2A), demonstrating the formation of CDs. UV–vis absorption spectra also confirmed the synthesis of CDs (Fig. S2B). SEM image in Fig. 1B showed that the DNA strands were woven together under the action of DNA polymerase to form a densely packed 3D DNA network structure in the shape of a nest, and these nest structures were of uniform size, proving the formation of DNA hydrogel. Atomic force microscopy (AFM) also characterized the morphology of the DNA hydrogel, as shown in Figs. S3A and a 3D network-like structure was observed. The height of the gel fibers was about 7 nm (Fig. S3B). Next, to verify the successful encapsulation of CDs in the DNA hydrogel, simple control experiments were performed. As shown in Fig. 1C. In the absence of primers or ssDNA1 and ssDNA2, higher absorbance values were observed, indicating that CDs were not successfully encapsulated. In contrast, when primers, ssDNA1 and ssDNA2 were added, the lowest absorbance intensity was generated due to the hydrogel formed by the second round of amplification. If the Cas12a/crRNA/HPV-16 complex was added on this basis, the absorbance value of the system was significantly increased because the target activated the trans-cleavage activity of Cas12a, and the hydrogel was destroyed. The fluorescence intensity test in Fig. 1D showed the same trend. Moreover, in the second round of RCA, with the sequential addition of secondary primers, incubation for more than 16 h, followed by centrifugation at 10000 rpm for 5 min, and after washing 5 times with PBS buffer, hydrogel clumps were observed in centrifuge tubes either with CDs inside (Fig. S4A, right) or without CDs (Fig. S4A, left). The centrifuge tubes were then illuminated under a UV lamp at 302 nm, and the DNA hydrogel encapsulated with CDs showed green fluorescence visible to the naked eye (Fig. S4B), once again demonstrating the formation of DNA hydrogel and the successful embedding of CDs. In addition, The CDs loading efficiency was surveyed using UV–vis assay (Fig. S5), and the results showed a ca. 7.8 % wt CDs loading.

To verify the successful implementation of the immobilization-free ECL sensing strategy, PAGE analysis was performed in Fig. 2 A. Lanes 1–4 (pictured on the left) validated the formation of circ-DNA. Compared with the primer (lane 1), the PC (lane 2), and the low-mobility band double-stranded ring structure (lane 3), the band in lane 4 showed further accelerated mobility after the addition of Exo I (cut the linear PC) and Exo III (cleavage primer), proving that the PC was successfully ligated to form the circ-DNA template. In the gel diagram on the right, a bright DNA hydrogel band was presented above lane 1, and to verify the cleavage of DNA hydrogel by Cas12a, we chose to use DNA hydrogel products without CDs as the non-specific substrate for Cas12a. In the presence of target HPV-16 DNA, lane 3 showed DNA hydrogel was degraded into fragments of different lengths, and obvious DNA bands appeared in the gel. Conversely, in the absence of the target HPV-16 DNA (lane 2), the DNA hydrogel degradation was blocked because Cas12a is inactive.

In addition, ECL measurements were carried out to verify the feasibility of the constructed biosensor (Fig. 2B, Fig. S6). Bare GE exhibited a lower signal (curve a). After co-incubation of Cas12a/crRNA, and CDs-DNA hydrogel mixtures in the presence of target HPV-16 DNA, a strong ECL signal (curve f) was observed when the supernatant was dropped on the surface of GE, which was attributed to the cleavage of the CDs-DNA hydrogel by Cas12a and the release of CDs. Conversely, the absence of any component resulted in a lower ECL signal. These results, together with gel electrophoresis analysis, validated the successful construction of a biosensor for HPV-16 DNA detection.

The release of CDs and the disassembly of the DNA network is a critical step in signal output (Fig. 3 B). Therefore, the disintegration of DNA hydrogels was further investigated by PAGE and fluorescence experiments based on feasible experimental principles. Specifically, in Fig. 3A, the degree of Cas12a cleavage of the hydrogel at different times was visually observed using PAGE images. The larger size of the DNA hydrogel band was shown above lane 1, and the DNA hydrogel was split into fragments of different sizes with the prolongation of lysis time (lanes 2–7). Simultaneously by the released CDs fluorescence, the target-responsive DNA network disassembly could be observed with the naked eye (Fig. 3C), and the fluorescence intensity of CDs monotonously increased with time (Fig. 3D). These results showed that CDs-DNA hydrogel was successfully prepared and could be cleaved by target-activated Cas12a to release the signaling unit.

Next, several important experimental parameters were optimized to obtain the best performance. Given that DNA hydrogel is critical for the loading of CDs, we initially explored the primer concentration affecting the formation of DNA hydrogel by ECL response values. That is, primers with final concentrations of 1–10 μM were added to the system for preparing CDs-DNA hydrogel to complete the preparation of hydrogel, and then the target-responsive CRISPR/Cas12a detection system was added to react at 37 °C for 1 h before ECL signal output. As shown in Fig. 4 A,as the primer DNA final concentration increased from 1 μM to 10 μM, the ECL intensity gradually increased and reached a plateau. Ultimately, primer DNA at a concentration of 5 μM to encapsulate CDs as much as possible was chosen. Subsequently, the concentration ratio of Cas12a to crRNA and the cleavage time of Cas12a were evaluated. An efficient Cas12a/crRNA duplex concentration is an important factor for obtaining satisfactory performance, therefore, the difference of trans-cleavage activity at different concentration ratios (final concentration, Cas12a: 50 nm, crRNA: 25 nm, 50 nm, 75 nm,100 nm, 125 nm) was explored. As illustrated in Fig. 4B, when the ratio was 1:2 (Cas12a: 50 nm, crRNA: 100 nm), the ECL signal value was maximized, hence the 1:2 Cas12a/RNA ratio was selected for use in the following experiments. In addition, the whole detection system containing the target was incubated at 37 °C for a certain period (30–70 min) before ECL measurement. Fig. 4C showed the ECL signal intensity gradually increased with the extension of time and reached a plateau after 60 min, while the reaction time of 70 min will lead to a slightly higher background signal. Therefore, 60 min was chosen as the appropriate incubation time. As illustrated in Fig. 4D, the cleavage effect was different at various incubation temperatures, indicating that optimal cleavage activity of Cas12a required a more suitable temperature environment to achieve a balance between efficiency and sensitivity, and 37 °C was the appropriate temperature for Cas12a cleavage.

To verify the detection capability of the ECL biosensor, the synthetic target HPV-16 DNA was detected at different concentrations ranging from 1 pM to 100 nM. As revealed by ECL measurements (Fig. 5 A), the ECL signal intensity was enhanced with increasing concentration of target DNA. Within the investigated concentration range, Δ ECL intensity (Δ EI, signal difference between target group and blank group) correlated well with the logarithm of target concentration (Fig. 5B), which was described by the linear regression equation Δ EI = 2693.39 lgC +33176.4 (R2 = 0.9952). The limit of detection (LOD) was 0.63 pM (detailed information was shown in Supporting Information). The detection of HPV-16 relied on the trans-cleavage ability of CRISPR/Cas12a as well as the release of CDs, which was nearly two orders of magnitude higher than traditional fluorescence-based amplification-free CRISPR/Cas12a diagnosis (Fig. S7). The reason may be that the hydrogel in a homogeneous solution is a network structure formed by the entanglement of DNA strands, which has a high local concentration, so it is conducive to improving the shearing efficiency of Cas12a protein. Moreover, in comparison to several previously published methods (Table S2), the constructed immobilization-free ECL sensing platform demonstrates better or comparable LOD for HPV-16 DNA.

To assess the specificity of ECL biosensors based on Cas12a and CDs-DNA hydrogel, we chose HPV-18, HPV-33, HPV-58, and HBV DNA as an interferer, and the relevant results in Fig. 5C showed that the Cas12a system has excellent specificity for the target HPV-16 DNA. Next, the repeatability of the constructed sensing platform was also investigated. That is, under the same experimental conditions, five parallel experiments were performed with different concentrations of target HPV-16 DNA (10 pM, 1 nM). In Fig. 5D–a low RSD of 2.65 % and 0.89 % was observed, which indicated that the ECL biosensor based on Cas12a and CDs-DNA hydrogel we constructed has remarkable reproducibility.

In addition, the stability of CDs-DNA hydrogel (stored at 4 °C refrigerator for 15 days) was further evaluated by using different concentrations of the target. The corresponding results were shown in Fig. S8, normal ECL signals were observed after incubation of CDs-DNA hydrogel with HPV-16 DNA/Cas12a/crRNA complex for 60 min. The results indicated that the CDs-DNA hydrogel we prepared has excellent stability and is conducive to the repeated construction of ECL biosensors.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Practical application of the constructed sensor
ce:section.ce:para: To verify the application ability of the constructed immobilization-free ECL sensing platform for the analysis of HPV-16 DNA in a complex biological matrix, different concentrations of target HPV-16 DNA (10 pM, 100 pM, 1 nM) were injected into 10-fold diluted serum of healthy people to prepare actual samples, and then the target in serum was detected by the constructed detection platform. As illustrated in Table S3, the recovery rate of HPV-16 DNA was 97.7–102 % (The calculation method is described in the Supporting Information), further demonstrating that our constructed ECL biosensor based on Cas12a and CDs-DNA hydrogel has certain clinical diagnostic potential.

In this work, we prepared a CDs embedded DNA hydrogel for CRISPR/Cas12a-responsive degradation to realize direct label/immobilization-free ECL sensing. Benefiting from the advantages of the ECL technique, the intrinsic properties of CRISPR/Cas12a, and the high loading efficiency of smart DNA hydrogel, we could achieve the detection of target HPV-16 DNA with high sensitivity and specificity without any label, immobilization, and pre-amplification steps. We also demonstrated its applicability by directly detecting HPV-16 DNA in human serum samples within 60 min from sample to answer. Overall, this label/immobilization-free sensor has several advantages in nucleic acid detection, including high sensitivity, simplicity, accuracy, and quick detection, and has the potential to enable a low-cost option for POC clinic diagnostics.

Honghong Wang: Writing – original draft, Visualization, Software, Project administration, Methodology, Conceptualization. Xiaomin Hang: Writing – review & editing, Visualization, Validation, Software. Huiyi Wang: Writing – review & editing, Visualization, Validation, Software. Jiaxin Peng: Writing – original draft, Visualization, Validation, Software. Haoming Yu: Software, Validation, Visualization, Writing – original draft. Li Wang: Data curation, Funding acquisition, Resources, Supervision, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research did not receive any specific grant from funding agencies in the public, commercial, or not-for-profit sectors.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.talanta.2024.126114.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Electrochemiluminescence systems for the detection of biomarkers: strategical and technological advances

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Sensitive and non-separation 5-hydroxymethylcytosine double-stranded DNA sensing in different mice tissues: electrogenerated chemiluminescence biosensing method incorporating DNA nanomachine amplification and nanocomposite magnetic separation
